messages.content:
<reword/rephrase"In a seller’s market, you can add about 10 percent to a comparable sale, since inventory is limited and buyers are competing for fewer homes.  
Neutral market: In a neutral real estate market, there’s an even balance between the number of buyers and the number of homes for sale. In this market, you’ll want to keep an eye on nearby comparables to make sure your pricing is similar.">

For a seller's market, due to limited inventory and high competition among buyers, you may consider adding approximately 10 percent to a comparable sale. On the other hand, a neutral real estate market entails a balance between the number of buyers and available homes for sale, prompting you to monitor nearby comparables to ensure your pricing aligns accordingly.
<reword/rephrase"Seller’s market: In a seller’s market, you can add about 10 percent to a comparable sale, since inventory is limited and buyers are competing for fewer homes. ">
One can enhance the price of a comparable sale by approximately 10 percent in a seller's market due to the limited inventory and high demand among buyers for fewer homes available.

<reword/rephrase"If you’re working with a real estate agent, they’ll be providing you with a CMA, which is a compilation of recent sales from your area. It takes into consideration home details, days on the market, and final sale price.

If you’re selling your home on your own, you can definitely do your own research online and get a good idea of your home’s value. But, don’t be afraid to contact a few real estate agents to  request a CMA. They’re used to providing comps to potential clients, and they may not even need to step foot in your home. You might also consider hiring an independent appraiser. For a few hundred dollars, they can give you a fair market value for your home."

If you have a real estate agent, they will likely provide you with a Comparative Market Analysis (CMA), which encompasses recent sales in your area by considering factors such as home features, days on market, and final sale price. In case you're selling the home independently, you could do your research online to approximate your home's value; however, it is recommended to reach out to a few real estate agents to request a CMA. These agents are accustomed to providing comps to potential clients and may not necessarily need to visit your home. Additionally, hiring an independent appraiser is another option to consider. For a few hundred dollars, they could evaluate your home and provide a fair market value.

"reword/rephrase"Remember, buyers have access to a lot of information about your home. With this information in hand, it is unlikely they will be taken in by a price above market value. They will also be able to see how long the home has remained on the market, and they may be prone to avoid the home the longer it remains unsold. The days of information only being available to real estate agents are long gone. Home buyers today are armed to the tooth with information. An educated home buyer understands when they see a good deal as much as an unrealistic seller who has pipe dreams about what their property is worth.">

It's essential to note that homebuyers now have access to ample information about your property, making it challenging to deceive them with an inflated price above the market value. Furthermore, they can check how long the property has been on the market and might avoid homes that have remained unsold for extended periods. In contrast to the past, real estate agents aren't the only ones with access to information. Modern homebuyers have become well-informed and can identify when they've spotted a good deal versus an unrealistic seller overestimating their property's value.

<reword/rephrase"Consider the price range your home will fall into on popular online real estate websites. Most buyers have a price range they are considering or can afford. A buyer looking at homes in the $280,000 to $300,000 range will likely not see your home if it’s listed at $305,000. But, if you choose a home listing price of $299,999, it’ll show up in their search results — and they just might end up being your buyer.">

It's crucial to factor in the price range your home would fall under on popular online real estate websites since most buyers selectively consider homes based on their affordability. Therefore, if a buyer's budget falls between $280,000 to $300,000, your home won't appear in their search results if listed above $305,000. However, by pricing it at $299,999, your home becomes visible in their results, increasing the probability of them potentially becoming the buyer for your property.

<reword/rephrase"Ever heard of price banding? It’s the practice of looking over current inventory in your neighborhood and finding a less crowded price point. Prices tend to get bunched up as sellers try to price their homes competitively. For example, if there are four homes in your neighborhood priced between $274,000 and $276,000, and the next set of homes start at $290,000 and up, you should take advantage of the wide open $280,000 price band."

Are you familiar with the concept of price banding? It involves scrutinizing the current inventory in your neighborhood to determine a less congested price point, as several sellers tend to advertise their properties competitively, resulting in clumped-up prices. For instance, suppose four homes in your neighborhood are priced between $274,000 and $276,000, whereas the next set of houses already start from $290,000 and above. In that case, it's appropriate to exploit the open $280,000 price band.

<reword/rephrase"Agents are pros when it comes to pricing properties and have their finger on the pulse of your local market. They understand current buying trends and can provide insight into how your home compares to others for sale nearby. It might be worth enlisting an agent’s help if you’re having trouble finding the right price point. Don’t have an agent? Zillow’s agent directory allows you to search for real estate experts in your area and includes reviews and other insights to help you compare and choose the agent who is right for you.">

Real estate agents are pricing specialists who are well-versed in the local market trends and can offer suggestions on how your property fares against others nearby. If you're struggling to determine the appropriate price point, it's advisable to consider seeking an agent's assistance. If you don't already have an agent, Zillow's agent directory is a resourceful tool that lets you search for reputable real estate professionals in your area. The directory comprises reviews and other insights to help you make an informed decision and choose an agent suitable for your needs.

<reword/rephrase"Many mistakes are consistently made with house pricing by using inaccurate methods. Pricing a house too high is commonplace when looking at data points that do not correlate with market value.">

Making pricing errors while evaluating a house is a common occurrence when imprecise methods are utilized. Frequently, data points used to fix a home's price are uncorrelated with the property's market value, causing homes to be consistently priced incorrectly on the higher end.

<reword/rephrase"Zillow Estimates Are Not Your Home's Market ValueOne of the banes of many professional real estate agents is house pricing based on looking at Zillow. Looking at an inaccurate Zillow estimate of value is commonplace.">

One of the issues faced by several real estate agents is determining house prices based on Zillow estimates, as they are not accurate at reflecting one's home's market value. Therefore, it's common for an inflated Zillow estimate to be used when pricing the property.

<reword/rephrase"The town or city you reside in will have an assessed value of your property. This value is how they figure out their tax structure. Assessed values, however, are nothing more than a measuring stick to collect an appropriate amount of taxes from those that reside in the community. In other words, how much does the town need to operate correctly? The official nature of this may encourage you to rely on the assessed value of your home for pricing purposes, which is almost always a big mistake. Assessed values almost always do not correlate with current market value.  One of the most significant problems with the assessed value is that it is often not current. Municipalities will usually assess value once every 1-3 years. The market can change substantially in six months, much less a year. Even some not-so-skilled real estate agents will fall for this one. Never look at tax assessments for house pricing.">

Your town or city will likely have an assessed value for your property, which is established to determine the tax structure. However, this value merely serves as a measuring tool to ensure that the appropriate amount of taxes is collected from residents and isn't indicative of a property's market value. In other words, the assessed value is determined on the basis of how much the town needs to function efficiently. Relying on the assessed value for pricing purposes, which is often an official process, could lead to a significant mistake. Generally, assessed values don't correlate with the current market value, and one of the common problems with them is that they aren't updated frequently. Municipalities typically evaluate values once every 1-3 years, whereas the market can change substantially in as little as six months. Even some inexperienced real estate agents make this mistake, emphasizing the need to steer clear of tax assessments when evaluating a property's price.

<reword/rephrase"It may come as a surprise, but not all home renovations have a positive ROI. In fact, Remodeling Magazine reported that the average payback for 22 common professional remodeling projects in 150 major U.S. markets was only about 62% in 2022. That means your renovations likely won’t translate into a dollar-for-dollar increase in home value. So, if your bathroom remodel cost about $27,000, you may see a resale value of just about $16,000.">

Although it may seem counterintuitive, not every home renovation guarantees a positive Return on Investment (ROI). Remodeling Magazine's report in 2022 indicated that the average payback for 22 common professional remodeling projects over 150 major U.S. markets was just around 62%. In simpler terms, renovations are unlikely to yield a dollar-for-dollar increase in home value. For instance, if your bathroom remodel costs $27,000, the resale value may only see a surge of roughly $16,000.
